enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted platform where a group takes turns queuing and playing music or video together in real time.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: üWave is an open-source, self-hosted platform for listening to media together as a group. Participants join a shared room and take turns adding songs, talks, or videos to a queue (the 'waitlist'), pulling content from source plugins for services like YouTube and SoundCloud.

The project is built as a Node.js/React stack, with the core server and web client published under the u-wave GitHub organization. There is no official managed SaaS version — running üWave means deploying your own server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Communities, friend groups, and internet-radio-style collectives who want a shared, turn-based listening room they fully control, rather than relying on a third-party service.

features.title: Turn-based queue
features.description: A waitlist system lets each participant take a turn choosing what plays next.

features.title: Source plugins
features.description: Officially supports pulling media from YouTube and SoundCloud, with a plugin system for adding more sources.

features.title: Live chat
features.description: Built-in chat runs alongside playback so the group can talk while listening.

features.title: Self-hosted core server
features.description: The core library exposes a REST/WebSocket API that the web client and third-party bots can use.

features.title: Web-based client
features.description: A React-based web client provides the room, queue, and chat interface.

features.title: Extensible plugin architecture
features.description: Developers can write custom source plugins beyond the officially supported ones.

pros: Free and open source under the MIT license
Full control over hosting and data since it's entirely self-hosted
Supports multiple content sources via plugins
Extensible architecture for developers
Active GitHub organization with separate core/web/hub repos
cons: Requires self-hosting know-how (Node.js/MongoDB stack)
No official managed or paid hosting option
Documentation is aimed at developers, not end users
UI is functional rather than polished compared to commercial listening apps

faqs.question: Is üWave free?
faqs.answer: Yes, üWave is open source under the MIT license and free to self-host.

faqs.question: Do I need to host üWave myself?
faqs.answer: Yes. üWave is self-hosted software with no official managed SaaS version, though independent public instances sometimes exist.

faqs.question: What content sources does üWave support?
faqs.answer: Officially YouTube and SoundCloud via source plugins, with support for adding custom plugins.

faqs.question: What tech stack powers üWave?
faqs.answer: The core server and web client are built with Node.js and React; the source is on GitHub under the u-wave organization.

use_cases.title: Community listening rooms
use_cases.description: Groups queue up music or videos and take turns playing them in a shared room.

use_cases.title: Internet radio-style collectives
use_cases.description: Friends or fan communities run a persistent shared queue as an informal internet radio station.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: üWave is an open-source, self-hosted platform for groups to queue and listen to music or video together in real time.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source streaming server that restreams live video from your own cameras or encoders to your site and platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Restreamer, built by datarhei, is a free, Apache-2.0-licensed Docker application for turning an H.264 video source — an IP camera, USB webcam, RaspiCam, or hardware encoder — into an HLS stream you can embed on your own website. It can simultaneously push the same feed to platforms like YouTube, Twitch, Facebook, or Vimeo, or to any generic RTMP server.
Because it runs entirely on your own Docker-capable infrastructure, there's no dependency on a third-party streaming provider or recurring subscription fee.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Broadcasters, security/CCTV operators, hobbyist streamers, and event producers who want to simulcast a live feed to multiple platforms without paying for a commercial multistreaming service.

features.title: Multi-destination restreaming
features.description: Push one source feed to YouTube, Twitch, Facebook, Vimeo, or any RTMP destination at the same time.

features.title: HLS output with HTML5 player
features.description: Serves an H.264/HLS stream with a built-in HTML5 video player for embedding on your own site.

features.title: Broad input support
features.description: Accepts IP cameras, USB cameras, RaspiCam, and H.264 hardware encoders as sources.

features.title: Browser-based configuration
features.description: A password-protected web UI handles setup without needing the command line for daily use.

features.title: Audio transcoding
features.description: Converts audio to AAC or MP3 as needed for output compatibility.

features.title: Scheduled snapshots
features.description: Captures still-frame snapshots from the stream at configurable intervals.

features.title: JSON HTTP API
features.description: Exposes an API for automating configuration and stream management.

features.title: Runs almost anywhere
features.description: Deploys via Docker on Linux, Windows, macOS, Raspberry Pi, and other ARM devices.

pros: Completely free and open source (Apache 2.0)
No per-stream or bandwidth fees since it's self-hosted
Simulcasts to multiple platforms simultaneously
Lightweight enough to run on a Raspberry Pi
Browser-based setup with no command-line required day to day
cons: Requires Docker and basic server/networking knowledge to deploy
You're responsible for your own uptime, bandwidth, and updates
Limited to H.264 input sources
Smaller ecosystem and support base than commercial streaming platforms

faqs.question: Is Restreamer really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's open source under the Apache 2.0 license with no license fees; you only pay for the server and bandwidth you use to host it.

faqs.question: What platforms can Restreamer send my stream to?
faqs.answer: It can simultaneously restream to your own website plus destinations like YouTube, Twitch, Facebook, Vimeo, or any generic RTMP server.

faqs.question: Do I need special hardware?
faqs.answer: No — it accepts input from IP cameras, USB webcams, RaspiCam, or any H.264 encoder, and runs on Docker-capable hardware including Raspberry Pi.

faqs.question: Does Restreamer have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, it exposes a JSON HTTP API for automating configuration and stream management.

use_cases.title: Live camera restreaming
use_cases.description: Take an IP camera feed and publish it as HLS on your own website without a third-party provider.

use_cases.title: Multi-platform live simulcasting
use_cases.description: Push one source feed to YouTube, Twitch, and Facebook at once without a paid multistreaming SaaS.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Restreamer is a free, open-source, self-hosted server for restreaming live video from your own cameras or encoders to your website and platforms.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source YouTube-style video sharing app written in Go with automatic transcoding.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Tube is a lightweight, self-hosted video-sharing application written in Go by developer James Mills (prologic), maintained on his self-hosted Git instance at git.mills.io. It bills itself as a minimal YouTube-style alternative you run yourself, with a built-in FFmpeg-based pipeline that automatically transcodes uploads to MP4 (H.264/AAC) and generates thumbnails.
Unlike most video platforms, Tube needs no external database — video metadata is read directly from files on disk — which keeps deployment simple.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Individuals or small communities who want a no-frills, self-hosted place to publish videos without relying on YouTube or maintaining a database-heavy platform.

features.title: Automatic transcoding
features.description: Uploads are automatically transcoded to MP4 H.264/AAC via a built-in FFmpeg pipeline.

features.title: Automatic thumbnails
features.description: Thumbnail images are generated automatically for uploaded videos.

features.title: No database required
features.description: Video metadata is derived directly from the filesystem instead of a database.

features.title: Multiple collections
features.description: Videos can be organized into separate collections.

features.title: RSS feeds
features.description: Each collection can generate an RSS feed for followers.

features.title: Minimal-JS interface
features.description: A mostly-HTML UI with graceful upload degradation keeps the client lightweight.

features.title: Optional upload password
features.description: The /upload endpoint can be protected with a password via an environment variable.

pros: Very lightweight — no external database to manage
Simple deployment via Docker image or Go binary
Open source under the MIT license
Actively maintained by its author
RSS feeds make collections easy to follow
cons: Small, single-maintainer project rather than a large community platform
Fewer features than full video platforms (no built-in comments or subscriptions)
Sparse official releases (only a couple of tagged versions)
Hosted primarily on the author's own Gitea instance rather than GitHub

faqs.question: Is Tube free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's open source under the MIT license and free to self-host.

faqs.question: Does Tube require a database?
faqs.answer: No — it reads video metadata directly from the filesystem, which simplifies deployment.

faqs.question: How do I deploy Tube?
faqs.answer: Via Docker (a prologic/tube image is published) or by building the Go binary from source.

faqs.question: Can I restrict who uploads videos?
faqs.answer: Yes, the /upload endpoint can be protected with a password set via an environment variable.

use_cases.title: Personal video hosting
use_cases.description: Host and share personal or family videos outside of YouTube.

use_cases.title: Community video archives
use_cases.description: Small open-source projects or communities publish videos with RSS syndication.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Tube is a free, open-source, self-hosted video-sharing app written in Go with automatic transcoding, thumbnails, and RSS feeds and no database required.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, cross-platform media player that plays virtually any video or audio file with no extra codec packs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: VLC media player, developed by the non-profit VideoLAN project, is one of the most widely used free and open-source multimedia players. It plays most multimedia files, discs, webcams, devices, and network streams without needing separate codec packs, and runs on desktop and mobile platforms alike.
The project describes itself as completely free of ads, spyware, and user tracking, and its code is released under open-source licenses that vary by component.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Anyone who needs a free, reliable player that plays almost any file format across Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and iOS — from casual users to IT departments avoiding codec-pack licensing headaches.

features.title: Broad format support
features.description: Plays most codecs — MPEG-2, MPEG-4, H.264, MKV, WebM, WMV, MP3, and more — with no codec packs needed.

features.title: Plays discs, devices, and streams
features.description: Handles DVDs, CDs, VCDs, webcams, capture devices, and network streaming protocols.

features.title: Cross-platform
features.description: Available for Windows, macOS, Linux and other Unix-like systems, Android, and iOS.

features.title: Skinnable interface
features.description: Supports custom skins via a skin editor plus a library of extensions.

features.title: No ads or tracking
features.description: The player ships with no ads, spyware, or user tracking.

features.title: Actively maintained
features.description: Receives regular updates, with the 3.0.x branch still shipping security and bug-fix releases.

pros: Completely free with no ads or tracking
Extremely broad format and codec support out of the box
Available on nearly every major desktop and mobile platform
Open source and community-driven
Lightweight installer and low resource use
cons: Interface feels dated compared to some modern media players
Advanced features like skins and extensions require manual setup
Built for single-user playback, not team or enterprise media management

faqs.question: Is VLC really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, VLC is completely free, open source, and has no ads, spyware, or tracking.

faqs.question: What platforms does VLC run on?
faqs.answer: Windows, macOS, Linux and other Unix-like systems, Android, iOS, and more.

faqs.question: Do I need to install codec packs for VLC?
faqs.answer: No, VLC bundles support for most codecs (MPEG-2, MPEG-4, H.264, MKV, WebM, WMV, MP3, and more) out of the box.

faqs.question: Who develops VLC?
faqs.answer: VLC is developed by VideoLAN, a non-profit organization, and its source code is released under open-source licenses.

use_cases.title: Everyday media playback
use_cases.description: Play nearly any downloaded or streamed video/audio file on desktop or mobile.

use_cases.title: IT and education deployments
use_cases.description: Provide a free, license-cost-free player for varied media formats across school or office devices.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: VLC is a free, open-source, cross-platform media player from VideoLAN that plays nearly any video or audio format with no codec packs or tracking.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted toolbox for bookmark collections, encrypted image uploads, and link shortening.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Anchr, created by developer Ferdinand Mütsch (GitHub: muety), is an open-source 'toolbox for tiny tasks on the internet' that combines a bookmark manager, an image hosting service, and a link shortener in one self-hosted app. Bookmarks can be organized into collections with descriptions to make them easier to search.

The image upload feature supports client-side encryption, so uploaded photos are unreadable to anyone without the chosen password — a feature the project highlights as a privacy differentiator from typical image hosts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Privacy-conscious individuals who want to self-host their bookmarks, image sharing, and short links instead of relying on separate third-party services.

features.title: Bookmark collections
features.description: Save links with descriptions and organize them into categorized collections for easier searching.

features.title: Encrypted image uploads
features.description: Upload one or more photos with optional client-side password encryption so no one else can view them.

features.title: Link shortening
features.description: Generate short links for any URL.

features.title: Browser extension
features.description: A Chrome extension lets users save or shorten links directly from the page they're viewing.

features.title: Self-hostable
features.description: Deploy your own instance from the open-source muety/anchr repository, backed by MongoDB.

features.title: Notification integrations
features.description: Supports email/SMTP and Telegram notification configuration.

pros: Combines bookmarks, image hosting, and link shortening in one app
Genuine client-side image encryption option for privacy
Free and open source
Lightweight, fast interface
cons: Requires self-hosting and MongoDB setup for full control
Smaller project with less frequent updates than larger bookmark managers like Linkding or Linkwarden
Limited official documentation beyond the GitHub repository

faqs.question: Is Anchr free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Anchr is open source and free to self-host; the code is available on GitHub under muety/anchr.

faqs.question: Can I use Anchr without self-hosting it?
faqs.answer: The official anchr.io instance is publicly accessible, though the project is primarily designed to be self-hosted.

faqs.question: Does Anchr encrypt uploaded images?
faqs.answer: Yes, it offers client-side password encryption so images are unreadable to anyone without the password.

faqs.question: What database does Anchr use?
faqs.answer: Anchr is backed by MongoDB.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted bookmark sync
use_cases.description: Keep a bookmark collection accessible from any device without relying on browser sync.

use_cases.title: Private image sharing
use_cases.description: Share photos with password-based client-side encryption instead of a third-party image host.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Anchr is a free, open-source, self-hosted toolbox combining bookmark collections, encrypted image uploads, and link shortening.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted proof-of-work reverse proxy that blocks AI scraper bots before they reach your site.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Anubis is an open-source, MIT-licensed reverse proxy created by developer Xe Iaso and now maintained by their company, Techaro. It sits in front of a website and requires each visitor's browser to solve a small SHA-256 proof-of-work challenge before serving a page. Legitimate human visitors barely notice the brief delay, while high-volume AI scrapers that ignore robots.txt face a steep, deliberately expensive computational cost for every page crawled.

The project was built after an AI crawler overwhelmed the author's own self-hosted Git server, and has since been adopted by projects including GNOME GitLab, the Linux kernel mailing list archive, FFmpeg, and Wine.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Open-source projects, self-hosted Git/forge instances, and any website operator being hit by aggressive AI/LLM training crawlers who want a free, self-hosted alternative to commercial bot-mitigation services.

features.title: Proof-of-work challenge
features.description: Unrecognized visitors must solve a SHA-256 puzzle before being granted access to the site.

features.title: Known-bot allowlist
features.description: Configurable allowlist recognizes crawlers like Google, Bing, and DuckDuckGo via user-agent and reverse-DNS verification.

features.title: Persistent auth cookie
features.description: Verified visitors get a cookie so they aren't re-challenged for roughly a week.

features.title: Reverse-proxy deployment
features.description: Runs as a lightweight proxy in front of any existing origin server.

features.title: Open-source core
features.description: MIT-licensed and self-hostable via Docker or standalone binaries.

features.title: Optional reputation add-on
features.description: A paid service called Thoth adds GeoIP and BGP ASN-based reputation filtering on top of the free core.

pros: Free and open source at its core
Effective, low-friction deterrent against high-volume scraping
Simple reverse-proxy deployment in front of existing infrastructure
Widely adopted by well-known open-source infrastructure projects
Actively maintained
cons: Adds a JavaScript challenge step that can affect users with JS disabled or certain accessibility tools
Advanced GeoIP/reputation filtering requires the paid Thoth add-on
Requires running and maintaining an additional proxy component
May occasionally challenge legitimate bots not on the allowlist

faqs.question: Is Anubis free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core Anubis proxy is open source under the MIT license and free to self-host; an optional paid add-on called Thoth adds GeoIP/ASN reputation filtering.

faqs.question: How does Anubis stop AI crawlers?
faqs.answer: It makes each visitor's browser solve a small proof-of-work puzzle, which is trivial for occasional human visitors but computationally expensive at the scale AI scrapers operate.

faqs.question: Does Anubis block search engines like Google?
faqs.answer: No, it includes a configurable allowlist for known-good crawlers such as Google, Bing, and DuckDuckGo, verified by user-agent and reverse DNS.

faqs.question: Who maintains Anubis?
faqs.answer: It was created by developer Xe Iaso and is maintained by their company, Techaro.

use_cases.title: Protecting self-hosted forges
use_cases.description: Shield self-hosted Git or wiki instances from bandwidth-draining AI scraper traffic.

use_cases.title: General site protection
use_cases.description: Shield any origin server from aggressive LLM-training crawlers without paying for a commercial bot-management service.

features: GeoIP and BGP ASN-based reputation filtering
Paid add-on to the free Anubis core

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Anubis is a free, open-source, self-hosted proof-of-work reverse proxy that blocks AI scraper bots while letting real visitors and search engines through.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source terminal session recorder with a free hosted platform and a self-hostable server for saving recordings.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: asciinema is a free and open-source tool for recording terminal sessions as lightweight, text-based recordings — rather than video — and sharing them on the web. The command-line recorder captures a session with a single `asciinema rec` command, and recordings can be played back, embedded in blogs or docs, or streamed live.

The companion asciinema-server, built with Elixir and Phoenix, powers the free public asciinema.org platform and can also be self-hosted, giving teams full control over where their recordings are stored.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Developers, sysadmins, and technical writers who want to record and share terminal demos, tutorials, or CLI documentation without recording actual screen video.

features.title: One-command recording
features.description: Start recording a terminal session with a single `asciinema rec` command.

features.title: Text-based recording format
features.description: Recordings are saved as a small .cast text format rather than video, keeping file sizes tiny.

features.title: Free public hosting
features.description: asciinema.org offers free hosting and sharing for recordings out of the box.

features.title: Self-hostable server
features.description: The open-source asciinema-server can be deployed independently for private, self-hosted recording archives.

features.title: Embeddable player
features.description: Recordings can be embedded in blogs, documentation sites, and presentations.

features.title: Live streaming
features.description: Supports streaming a live terminal session in addition to recording and replay.

pros: Completely free and open source, both CLI and server
Recordings are tiny, text-based files instead of video
Viewers can select and copy text directly out of a recording
Fully self-hostable for teams that want data privacy
Simple one-command recording workflow
cons: Only records terminal/text output, not GUI applications
Self-hosting the server requires Elixir/Phoenix and PostgreSQL setup
Playback styling is limited compared to full screen-recording tools

faqs.question: Is asciinema free?
faqs.answer: Yes, both the CLI recorder and the asciinema.org hosting platform are free, and the underlying software is open source.

faqs.question: Can I self-host asciinema instead of using asciinema.org?
faqs.answer: Yes, the open-source asciinema-server project can be deployed via Docker Compose with PostgreSQL, and the CLI can be pointed at your own server via the ASCIINEMA_SERVER_URL variable.

faqs.question: What format are asciinema recordings in?
faqs.answer: Recordings are saved as a text-based .cast format rather than video, keeping file sizes small and allowing text to be selected and copied during playback.

faqs.question: Can I embed asciinema recordings elsewhere?
faqs.answer: Yes, recordings can be embedded in blogs, documentation sites, and presentations.

use_cases.title: CLI tutorials and docs
use_cases.description: Record terminal walkthroughs and embed them directly in documentation sites.

use_cases.title: Private team recording archive
use_cases.description: Self-host a team's internal terminal-recording server for sensitive workflows.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: asciinema is a free, open-source tool for recording and sharing terminal sessions as lightweight text recordings, with a self-hostable server option.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted amateur radio logging app for tracking QSOs, awards, and CAT-controlled radio contacts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Cloudlog is an open-source, PHP/MySQL web application for logging amateur radio contacts (QSOs), started by Peter Goodhall (MM9SQL) and developed further by a community of contributors on GitHub. It covers logging from HF through microwave, integrates with CAT control and tools like WSJT-X, and syncs with Logbook of the World (LoTW) for automated upload and award matching (DXCC, WAS, VUCC, IOTA, DOK, SOTA, and more).
Operators can either self-host Cloudlog on their own PHP/MySQL server or use a paid managed hosting subscription offered by the maintainer, MagicBug.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Licensed amateur radio ('ham') operators who want a modern, browser-accessible logbook they can reach from any device, either self-hosted or via managed hosting.

features.title: Web-based QSO logging
features.description: Log contacts from any device through a browser-accessible logbook.

features.title: CAT control integration
features.description: Connects to radios via CAT control for streamlined logging.

features.title: WSJT-X and satellite sync
features.description: Automatically syncs logs from WSJT-X and integrates with SatPC32 for satellite operators.

features.title: Logbook of the World integration
features.description: Automates LoTW upload and award matching without requiring TQSL.

features.title: Award tracking
features.description: Tracks DXCC, WAS, VUCC, IOTA, DOK, SOTA, and other operating awards.

features.title: Self-hosted or managed hosting
features.description: Run it yourself on PHP 7+/MySQL/MariaDB, or subscribe to managed hosting from MagicBug.

pros: Free and open source for self-hosting
Actively developed with ongoing community contributions
Broad award-tracking and CAT/WSJT-X integration
Optional managed hosting removes the need to run your own server
cons: Self-hosting requires a PHP/MySQL server and some technical setup
Managed hosting is a paid subscription
UI and feature depth trail some commercial ham-logging suites
Project relies on volunteer development time and donations

faqs.question: Is Cloudlog free?
faqs.answer: The source code is free and open source on GitHub, so self-hosting costs nothing beyond your own server. The maintainer also offers optional managed hosting starting around £4/month.

faqs.question: What do I need to self-host Cloudlog?
faqs.answer: A web server with PHP 7.0+ and MySQL or MariaDB.

faqs.question: Does Cloudlog integrate with Logbook of the World?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports automated LoTW upload and award matching without requiring TQSL.

faqs.question: Can Cloudlog control my radio?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports CAT control integration and can sync logs automatically from WSJT-X.

use_cases.title: Personal ham radio logbook
use_cases.description: Keep a self-hosted logbook accessible from phone, tablet, or PC.

use_cases.title: Contest and DXpedition logging
use_cases.description: Log contacts with automated award tracking during contests or DX trips.

features: Run on your own PHP/MySQL server

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cloudlog is a free, open-source, self-hosted amateur radio logging app with CAT control, WSJT-X sync, and Logbook of the World integration.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, standards-based printing system that manages local and network printers on macOS, Linux, and other Unix-like systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: CUPS (Common Unix Printing System) is the standards-based, open-source printing system developed by Apple for macOS, iOS, and iPadOS, and made available for other Unix-like operating systems through the OpenPrinting project. It uses IPP Everywhere for driverless printing to modern local and network printers, and exposes a browser-based administration interface for managing print queues.
Licensing depends on version: CUPS 2.2 and earlier used GNU GPL2/LGPL2 with exceptions, while CUPS 2.3 and later moved to the Apache License 2.0.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Linux/Unix system administrators and Apple platform engineers who need a standards-based print server for managing local or network printers.

features.title: IPP Everywhere support
features.description: Enables driverless printing to most modern local and network printers.

features.title: Web-based administration
features.description: A browser-based interface (typically at localhost:631) manages printers and queues.

features.title: Local and network printing
features.description: Supports both USB-connected and network-attached printers.

features.title: Print queue management
features.description: Handles job scheduling and queue administration.

features.title: Cross-platform availability
features.description: Built into macOS/iOS/iPadOS, and packaged for Linux and other Unix-likes via OpenPrinting.

pros: Free, open source, and standards-based (IPP)
Built into macOS/iOS, so no extra install needed on Apple platforms
Widely packaged across Linux distributions
Driverless printing works with most modern printers
cons: Setup and troubleshooting can require command-line/config-file familiarity
The version tracked on cups.org lags behind actively developed OpenPrinting forks
Not aimed at non-technical end users

faqs.question: Is CUPS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, CUPS is free and open source, licensed under the Apache License 2.0 for versions 2.3 and later (earlier versions used GPL2/LGPL2 with exceptions).

faqs.question: Who develops CUPS?
faqs.answer: It was developed by Apple for macOS, iOS, and iPadOS, with OpenPrinting maintaining versions for other operating systems.

faqs.question: What is IPP Everywhere?
faqs.answer: It's a driverless printing standard CUPS uses so many modern printers work without installing a manufacturer-specific driver.

faqs.question: Can I administer CUPS from a browser?
faqs.answer: Yes, CUPS includes a web-based administration interface for managing printers and print jobs.

use_cases.title: Shared network print server
use_cases.description: Set up a print server on Linux that serves printers to an entire network.

use_cases.title: Built-in Apple printing
use_cases.description: Manage local and network printers on macOS, iOS, and iPadOS under the hood.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: CUPS is the free, open-source, standards-based printing system behind macOS, iOS, and many Linux distributions, with IPP Everywhere driverless printing.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hostable collaborative online whiteboard for real-time classroom or team brainstorming.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Digiboard, by the French edtech platform LaDigitale, is a free, real-time collaborative online whiteboard. Multiple users can draw, annotate, and organize content together on the same canvas simultaneously, which makes it popular for classroom brainstorming and remote team sessions.
It's available as a free hosted tool at digiboard.app, and its source code is published on Codeberg under the AGPL-3.0 license for anyone who wants to self-host it on their own Node.js server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Teachers and students who need a free collaborative whiteboard for remote or in-class lessons, and teams that want a self-hostable brainstorming canvas.

features.title: Real-time collaboration
features.description: Multiple users can draw and edit the same canvas simultaneously.

features.title: Drawing and annotation tools
features.description: Provides tools for drawing, annotating, and organizing content on the board.

features.title: Cross-device support
features.description: Works on computers, tablets, and smartphones.

features.title: Free hosted version
features.description: Usable immediately at digiboard.app with no self-hosting required.

features.title: Self-hostable
features.description: Deploy your own instance as a Node.js app with Redis, including domain, port, and reverse-proxy configuration.

features.title: Open-source license
features.description: Source is published on Codeberg under the AGPL-3.0 copyleft license.

pros: Completely free, both hosted and self-hosted
Genuine real-time multi-user collaboration
Works across devices without installation
Open source under a copyleft license so improvements stay open
Straightforward Node.js/Redis deployment for self-hosters
cons: Self-hosting requires Node.js and Redis configuration
Primarily documented and marketed for French-language education use
Fewer advanced diagramming features than dedicated whiteboard suites

faqs.question: Is Digiboard free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free to use at digiboard.app and free and open source (AGPL-3.0) to self-host.

faqs.question: Who makes Digiboard?
faqs.answer: It's developed by LaDigitale, a French educational technology platform.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Digiboard?
faqs.answer: Yes, the source code is available on Codeberg (ladigitale/digiboard) and runs on Node.js with Redis.

faqs.question: What license is Digiboard released under?
faqs.answer: The AGPL-3.0 license.

use_cases.title: Classroom brainstorming
use_cases.description: Teachers and students collaborate on a shared visual canvas during remote or in-class lessons.

use_cases.title: Team ideation sessions
use_cases.description: Remote teams brainstorm together on a shared collaborative canvas.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Digiboard is a free, open-source collaborative online whiteboard by LaDigitale, usable hosted or self-hosted (AGPL-3.0) for real-time brainstorming.
